items.correct: An undifferentiated cell capable of dividing to produce more stem cells or differentiating into specialised cell types.
items.misconception: Stem cells are taken only from embryos and cannot be found in adults.
items.random_wrong: Stem cells are simply cells that haven't grown big enough yet.
items.why: Media coverage of embryonic stem cells overshadows adult stem cells, so students think 'stem cell' = 'embryo cell'.

items.q: What is the key structural difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?
items.correct: Eukaryotes have a membrane-bound nucleus and membrane-bound organelles; prokaryotes do not.
items.misconception: Prokaryotes have no DNA — that's why they have no nucleus.
items.random_wrong: Prokaryotes are always smaller than 1 micrometre and have no cytoplasm.
items.why: Students see 'no nucleus' and think 'no DNA', missing that prokaryotes still have DNA — it's just free in the cytoplasm.

items.q: What is the main role of mitochondria in a cell?
items.correct: To carry out aerobic respiration, producing ATP that cells use as an energy currency.
items.misconception: Mitochondria produce energy by creating it from nothing.
items.random_wrong: Mitochondria store sunlight and release it for photosynthesis.
items.why: The phrase 'powerhouse of the cell' makes students think energy is created by mitochondria, missing that they transfer energy from glucose to ATP.
The answer examiners want is: Image size ÷ real size. “Real size ÷ image size” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's 1000. “10” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Cell 0.04 mm long appears 8 mm. Magnification?
items.why: The real figure is 200. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
